coredata.dc:title: An overview of the recent advances of carbon quantum dots/metal oxides in the application of heterogeneous photocatalysis in photodegradation of pollutants towards visible-light and solar energy exploitation
coredata.dc:creator: Heng, Zeng Wei
Chong, Woon Chan
Pang, Yean Ling
Koo, Chai Hoon
coredata.dc:description: Since the discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), their potential applications in the fields of chemical sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery and photocatalysis have been enormously developed. In particular, CQDs have received immense attention in visible-light-driven heterogeneous photocatalysis for the sake of their excellent aqueous solubility, outstanding electron transfer and efficient light-harvesting ability that enable the full utilization of solar spectrum. Recently, green CQDs have attracted considerable interest in this burgeoning field due to their non-toxic nature, high sustainability, outstanding photostability and their innate photoluminescence properties. By virtue of the high biocompatibility and tunable optical properties, the applications of CQDs alone or in combination with other bulk semiconductors in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants have been reported in many studies. This review aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the recent progress and breakthroughs made in the development of diverse CQDs/semiconductor hybrid photocatalysts. The detailed information regarding their photocatalytic degradation performances and the mechanisms underlying the interaction of CQDs in photocatalytic system are highlighted. The current review also presents the strategies employed in the design of efficient photocatalyst with greater emphasis on the experimental condition, surface modification, facet control, charge transfer mode and the construction of ternary heterostructures. The resulting impact on the physiochemical properties and catalytic performance of hybrid photocatalysts could provide readers an insight into the recent advances made and stimulate innovative techniques which ultimately lead to significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity in solar energy conversion.

head.ce:title: An overview of the recent advances of carbon quantum dots/metal oxides in the application of heterogeneous photocatalysis in photodegradation of pollutants towards visible-light and solar energy exploitation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Lee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science, Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman, Sungai Long Campus, Jalan Sungai Long, Cheras, 43000 Kajang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), their potential applications in the fields of chemical sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery and photocatalysis have been enormously developed. In particular, CQDs have received immense attention in visible-light-driven heterogeneous photocatalysis for the sake of their excellent aqueous solubility, outstanding electron transfer and efficient light-harvesting ability that enable the full utilization of solar spectrum. Recently, green CQDs have attracted considerable interest in this burgeoning field due to their non-toxic nature, high sustainability, outstanding photostability and their innate photoluminescence properties. By virtue of the high biocompatibility and tunable optical properties, the applications of CQDs alone or in combination with other bulk semiconductors in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants have been reported in many studies. This review aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the recent progress and breakthroughs made in the development of diverse CQDs/semiconductor hybrid photocatalysts. The detailed information regarding their photocatalytic degradation performances and the mechanisms underlying the interaction of CQDs in photocatalytic system are highlighted. The current review also presents the strategies employed in the design of efficient photocatalyst with greater emphasis on the experimental condition, surface modification, facet control, charge transfer mode and the construction of ternary heterostructures. The resulting impact on the physiochemical properties and catalytic performance of hybrid photocatalysts could provide readers an insight into the recent advances made and stimulate innovative techniques which ultimately lead to significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity in solar energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the aggravating environmental problems of water pollution, solar energy conversion via photocatalysis has been grabbing major interest in water remediation since it can ensure sustainable development of wastewater treatment. Photocatalysis has been recognized as a “green” energy conversion technology in the context of clean and inexhaustible energy used [1–3]. The inherently destructive nature of photocatalysis plays a critical role in the mineralization of organic compounds [4]. Generally, the photocatalytic process relies on light absorption of semiconductor photocatalysts under ambient conditions [5]. The electron-hole pairs induced by light excitation will trigger and accelerate surface redox reactions for the structural destruction of toxic pollutants. To realize the full potential of photocatalytic reaction, an ideal photocatalyst is expected to achieve effective utilization of full-spectrum light and efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers [1]. Unfortunately, the present photocatalytic systems did not meet the prerequisite requirements due to their wide band gap nature and high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, hence resulting in limited photocatalytic performance [6]. Thus, band gap engineering of semiconductors is of great importance to produce an effective photocatalytic system with desired band gap.

In the past decades, much research on the development of carbonaceous-supported semiconductor has been attempted by combining bulk semiconductor with a diverse suite of carbonaceous materials such as activated carbon (AC) [7–9], graphene [10–12], carbon nanotube (CNTs) [13–16] and fullerenes [17–19]. Undeniably, the carbonaceous-based composites seem to tune the band gap of semiconductors for the development of visible light-active (VLA) photocatalysts [3,20,21]. Despite their outstanding adsorption capacities, the commercial-scale acceptance of these carbon-supported semiconductors is still limited due to the insufficient efficacy of visible light absorption and charge separation. Moreover, carbonaceous precursors such as CNTs, graphite and its derivative are mostly expensive and non-renewable in nature [22]. Henceforth, quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as a potential alternative to carbonaceous supports thanks to their tunable optoelectronic properties and potential applicability in a wide range of fields such as bioimaging [23,24], chemical sensing [25], drug delivery [26,27] and photocatalysis [5].

As a new discovery in nanotechnology, QDs are deemed as a zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterial with particle size below nanometer range [28,29]. Generally, semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) are nanocrystals that consist of a metalloid crystalline core covered by an organic layer [30]. The outer shell improves the luminescence properties and protects the semiconductor core from oxidation. However, the residues of the semiconductor QDs would contaminate the environment [31]. Another new class of nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged recently as a potential competitor to conventional semiconductor QDs due to the abundance of sustainable sources and facile green synthesis method [32]. Quantum dots are well-known for their luminescence characteristics thanks to the prominent quantum confinement effect in the nanoworld [33,34]. When the particle size of QDs reduced to nanoscale, excitons will be confined in all three spatial dimensions and ultimately give rise to discrete quantum states. The restrained mobility of electrons thereby generates a large band gap that can inhibit the recombination events which cause loss of active electrons [5]. Meanwhile, the high surface-area-to-volume ratio of QDs is accompanied by a higher sensitivity to light, hence light photons can be greatly absorbed by the nanoparticles. Apart from the size-dependent fluorescence of QDs, the optical properties of QDs also originate from the surface-state related luminescence induced by surface defects and functional groups of QDs. The chemically reactive surface states are attractive to a wide diversity of oxygen-containing moieties which eventually produce multi-color emissive QDs [35].

Although numerous review articles have been published on the application of CQDs-modified semiconductors in heterogeneous photocatalysis, an increasing number of studies on the combination of CQDs with a diverse range of bulk semiconductors have been extensively conducted in recent years. Therefore, it is essential to systematically collect and compile the increasing findings in a way that could provide a full picture of the current situation as well as the critical issues that have yet to be solved. In Section 2, we will highlight the green synthesis of CQDs-based composites regarding the natural precursors and synthetic technology applied in the synthesis process. In Section 3, we will discuss the photoluminescence origin of CQDs and provide an in-depth understanding of the working principle of luminescence CQDs. The modification efforts made in tailoring the band structure of photocatalysts will be studied as well. Moreover, the multifaceted roles of CQDs in boosting the photocatalytic activity of CQDs-supported semiconductor photocatalysts will be examined in Section 4.

To the best of our knowledge, there has been no detailed compilation of the application of different combinations of CQD-decorated semiconductor composites in visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation. Herein, this review summarizes the recent progress of the CQD-based hybrid materials and analyses the mechanisms behind the photocatalytic systems (Section 5). In this section, we put greater emphasis on the design parameters that have a major impact on physiochemical properties and catalytic performance of the hybrid photocatalysts. Finally, Section 6 provides an overview of current development, challenges encountered and future perspectives in shaping promising hybrid photocatalyst. It is believed that this organized collection of related information may contribute to a better understanding of photocatalyst design and spark innovative ideas for modification and improvement in the near future.

Among the members of the quantum dots family, carbon-based quantum dots have attracted great interest in photocatalysis due to their abundant nature, low toxicity, low cost and long lifetime [36]. Moreover, the unique properties of CQDs including superior aqueous solubility, chemical stability, good biocompatibility, ease of fabrication and functionalization have distinguished them from traditional semiconductor quantum dots [37,38]. Meanwhile, CQDs exhibit high resistance to photo-bleaching which inhibits photocorrosion under light irradiation, making them more photostable and durable than their metal-based counterparts [39].

There are two members of the carbon-based quantum dots family, which are carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [40]. CQDs are distinct quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm. They are dots made up of sp2 graphitic-core surrounded by sp3 carbon matrix filled with various functional groups. The carbogenic core and surface structure are responsible for the photoluminescence (PL) and upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties [41]. On the other hand, GQDs are nanoparticles in the size range of 2–20 nm. They are mainly composed of sp2 hybridized carbon surrounded by crystalline domains that exhibit better crystallinity than CQDs [41]. In contrast to graphene material with zero band gap, the distinct energy bands in GQDs can impart tunable fluorescence properties due to the pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects [39].

The CQDs nanoparticles can be synthesized from a wide variety of carbon precursor materials via different synthesis processes, as listed in Table 1. According to past researches, numerous chemical precursors have been reported to produce CQDs, such as citric acid [42], ascorbic acid [43], ammonium citrate [44] and glucose [45]. Among all the starting materials used, citric acid that mainly consists of hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups is the most popular raw material employed in CQDs fabrication [46]. Various synthesis techniques such as solvothermal [47], hydrothermal [48], microwave irradiation [42], ultrasonic [49] and thermal pyrolysis [50] have been implemented to make CQDs. Di et al. [50] reported the synthesis of CQDs from citric acid via the thermal pyrolysis method. They observed that the as-prepared CQDs were homogeneously distributed with a diameter at about 4 nm. Su et al. [49] fabricated CQDs using glucose as the carbon source via a facile alkali-assisted ultrasonic process. The starting material was broken down by the high energy of ultrasonic sound waves to form CQDs with superior up-converted photoluminescence property.

Recently, biomass-based CQDs have shown considerable potential as a green energy source that significantly contributes to sustainable photocatalytic technology. Following the use of natural precursors, green synthesis techniques have also been explored so that the overall production of CQDs works in a sustainable and affordable manner. As summarized in Table 1, CQDs have been synthesized through several synthetic routes which include hydrothermal, solvothermal, pyrolysis, microwave irradiation and thermal decomposition. Among all, hydrothermal synthesis is one of the most commonly used methods as it involves facile preparation routes, hence more economical [51]. It has been proclaimed that the selection of carbon precursors and synthetic methods require careful consideration as this may affect the complexity of carbon hybridization and surface properties of CQDs [35,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomass precursor and green synthesis technique of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, most studies have shown that food waste, plants and even by-products in petroleum refining consist of high carbon content. These low-grade carbonaceous wastes can potentially be transformed into high-value CQDs [70]. The newly emerging green carbon sources including biomass and food waste are listed in Table 2. Compared to the dense and well-ordered structure of bulk CNTs and graphene, the carbon-rich biomass (45–55 wt%) can be easily disintegrated without undergoes tedious and complex pretreatment steps [38]. This in turn eliminates the use of strong oxidizing agents and harsh operating conditions, leading to scalable production with high yields.

Recently, coal tar pitch was used as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CQDs [71]. The synthesized green CQD exhibited a graphitic structure with alkyl chains attached at the edge of nucleus which was similar to that of chemical-derived CQDs. Other researchers have used different cheap carbonaceous sources, such as petroleum coke, coal and coal-based humic acid to synthesize value-added carbon products [70]. These materials were evaluated for their chemical properties using proximate and ultimate analysis. The studies found that the carbon samples are rich in carbon with low ash content. The synthesized CQDs from the different carbonaceous samples have a diameter in the range of 3–6.5 nm and QY of about 2–22%. Besides, non-edible parts of plants have also been used to prepare green CQDs. Wang et al. [22] and Hasija et al. [72] prepared CDs using bamboo leaves as the green precursor. Tuna et al. [73] used Gingko biloba leaves to prepare CQDs by using microwave irradiation technique. Other plant sources, such as poplar wood [74], palm veneers [75] and waste larch wood [76] have also been utilized to synthesize CQDs. In addition to plant sources, researchers have also used food as precursors, such as mushroom [77], pomelo peel [78], vitamin C [79], as well as fruit juice such as pineapple [80] and sugarcane juice [81] to fabricate green CDs.

In Malaysia, palm oil plantation and mill industry generate a substantial amount of biomass wastes such as empty fruit bunches (EFB), oil palm fronds (OPF), oil palm trunks (OPT), oil palm leaves (OPL), palm kernel shell (PKS) and mesocarp fiber (MF) [82]. Although these wastes are known to contain high carbon and fiber content, the high potential value is often being neglected by industries and sectors [83]. Eventually, the residual waste may result in biomass overload and disposal problems. In view of this, a few have converted EFB [84,85] and oil palm frond [86] to value-added fluorescent CQD. Interestingly, these biomass-derived CQDs exhibit photoluminescence features which were comparable to other carbon and chemically-derived QDs (CNT, graphite, glucose, citric acid, etc) and the carbon waste exhibits a similar chemical structure to that of citric acid [87]. Besides, green CQDs are also cheaper, safer and scalable due to their wide availability, high renewability and nature-friendly benefits which are in line with the sustainable development goal (SDG). The utilization of renewable resources also minimizes industrial waste which is consistent with the concept of zero waste discharge.

Besides, the adoption of natural carbon materials also guarantees a high quantum efficiency of CQDs due to the self-passivation behavior of the natural carbon sources [87,88]. It was reported that raw CQDs contain some flaws in fluorescence intensity and quantum yield (QY) due to the presence of non-radiative defect states on the CQDs surface. Therefore, surface passivation and functionalization of bare CQDs are necessary to inactivate the surface dangling bonds that would lead to the non-radiative emission of CQDs [89]. Aghamali et al. [48] discovered that NCQDs synthesized from citric acid had attained an unbelievably high QY (95%) after surface passivation. The energy traps contributed by the doping of nitrogen elements can promote the recombination events through a radiative relaxation pathway. However, in the work of Liu et al. [90], it was found that the biomass-based CQDs without surface modification possess a higher QY than the chemical-derived CQDs due to the naturally existing nitrogen elements in them. Biomass wastes that rich in C˭N and C˭O functional groups in nature can enhance their quantum yield and luminescence properties of CQDs [52]. Despite the numerous advantages, the application of green CQDs in photocatalysis is still not well-established as compared to that of chemical-derived CQDs. Hence, additional exploration is required to develop environmentally-benign CQDs with remarkable optical and catalytic properties.

Besides replacing the starting material of CQDs, it is also important to develop a facile synthetic technique that can reduce energy consumption during the production process. In general, CQDs can be synthesized via two main approaches, which are the top-down and bottom-up methods [40,52]. Most natural carbon sources that consist of bioactive small-molecules require a series of processes to form carbon nanodots, which include condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [90]. The bottom-up approach is preferred by most researchers due to its simple preparation route, ease of operation, low cost and most importantly, it permits the use of a variety of inexpensive carbon materials [91,92]. Among all the bottom-up approaches, hydrothermal [93–96] and microwave irradiation [73,97–99] have drawn great attention in the synthesis of biomass-derived CQD (Table 2).

Hydrothermal treatment is a facile and green method that eliminates the use of strong acid agents and allows the carbonization, surface functionalization and passivation to be conducted in a single step [75]. Although the hydrothermal route can reduce the overall cost and complexity of the process, this treatment often requires a higher reaction temperature (greater than 150 °C) and longer operational time which usually takes several hours (8–12 h) for the carbonization process to complete [100]. Comparatively, microwave-assisted irradiation is a facile and low-cost method that can synthesis CQDs in a short period (only a few minutes) owing to the intensive and homogeneous heating of microwave irradiation [101]. This technique has been widely applied in numerous studies to synthesis CQDs in a very short time. For example, Tuna et al. and co-workers developed economical and time-saving microwave irradiation for the synthesis of CQD from Gingko biloba. The process was conducted under different irradiation power (400–800 W) at different time scale (1, 3, 5, 7, 10 min). From the PL spectra obtained, the synthesis time of 10 min showed the highest fluorescence intensity which was more effective for CQD synthesis. In another investigation, green microwave-assisted synthesis of CQDs from edible mushroom was reported by Sargin et al. Optimization study was performed to determine the optimal operating condition. According to the results, the prepared solution was irradiated for 7 min at 400 W. Despite the rapid process, Dager et al. [102] and Liu et al. [103] reported that this technique has poor control over the CQDs size and the prepared CQDs have reduced PL intensity and poor photostability, which is probably due to indirect energy transfer to the carbon precursor that resulted in the formation of large-sized CQDs.

Despite the recent advances in these technologies, each synthesis method still exhibits its own merits and faults. Therefore, the development of a simple, rapid, energy-efficient and cost-effective approach for the preparation of high-quality CQDs is urgently in need. Recently, several studies reported the combination of both methods to synthesize CQDs, which has been found to fulfill all the requirements. Yang et al. [104] reported a rapid and direct microwave-assisted hydrothermal route to synthesize NCQDs from xylan precursors at a temperature of 200 °C and a power of 200 W in just 10 min time. The synthesized NCQDs exhibited favorable photoluminescence properties and showed high tolerance to salts and metal ions. Similar method has also been applied by Du et al. [105] where NCQDs were obtained by heating the prepared solution at 220 °C at an input power of 800 W for 10 min. The N-CQDs prepared demonstrated strong red emission with a QY of 27.6% and high stability. These findings reported the excellent feasibility and potential use of this combined technique.

To date, the fluorescence behavior of CQDs has endowed these nanoparticles with versatile applicability in multiple areas especially in the field of chemical sensing and bio-imaging. Despite numerous achievements have been accomplished, the fundamental insight on the working mechanism of CQDs is not well-established. The fluorescence mechanism remains a mystery and has long been a subject of debate. The myth has garnered the attention of researchers to explore the fluorescence origin of CQDs and gain deeper insight into their tuning behavior. According to previous studies, the PL properties are closely related to the surface chemistry of CQDs. The electronic structure of CQDs can be engineered by tailoring the synthesis parameters such as carbon precursors, synthetic techniques, synthesis conditions and surface modification applied [110]. Despite all of the findings reported, researchers have failed to arrive at a uniform theory due to the intricate optical nature associated with complex structural characteristics of CQDs.

To date, two mainstream mechanisms have been accepted, which are the quantum confinement effect [111,112] and surface states emission [113–115]. The quantum confinement effect is an intrinsic state emission induced by band gap transition, depending on the size variation of π-conjugated core [89]. Conversely, surface states emission is an extrinsic state emission induced by the defect sites associated with a variety of functional groups [92,116]. In most cases, the observed luminescence of CQDs is originated from the synergistic emission of graphitic core and surface states [89,91,117]. The emission mechanisms are often interconnected as the graphitic domain can be governed by the evolution of surface functional groups on CQDs [90,91].

To have a better knowledge of the quantum confinement effect, it is important to first understand the chemical structure of CQDs in the nanometer regime. Generally, CQDs compose of π-conjugated sp2 core embedded within sp3 matrix [118]. According to Saikia et al. [70], the graphitic core structure of CQDs is the primary controlling factor of the quantum confinement effect. The photoluminescence of CQDs could be tuned (red or blue-shifting the emission band) by modulating the size of the sp2 domain since the band gap energy and the particle size of CQDs are highly intercorrelated.

As reported by Liu et al. [90], the PL emission band of CQDs can be blue-shifted by reducing the particle size. When the particle size decreases, the size of sp2 clusters reduces and the number of defects sites decreases as well. Since the sp2 domains are sites of localization for electron-hole pairs, such small domains would lead to confinement of excitons within the electronic structure, thereby increasing the π–π* energy band gap of CQDs. The quantum-confined electrons will release high-energy photons upon energy relaxation and thus emitting strong blue light. Contrarily, the increasing particle size of CQDs will form a larger graphitic domain with more defect sites. These defect sites would facilitate π-electron delocalization which gradually decreases the HOMO-LUMO gap. In addition, the increasing defect states might promote non-radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs, hence leading to frequent energy loss and resulting in low quantum yield of CQDs.

Ye et al. [112] suggested that the photoluminescence of GQDs was size-dependent and can be carefully controlled by regulating the temperature of chemical oxidation treatment. As expected, the emission peak was red-shifted when the particle size increased, which is in accordance with the quantum confinement effect. Fig. 1 shows the light emission of GQDs shifted from orange-red to blue-green as the oxidation temperature elevated from 50 °C to 150 °C. This is because, at a higher temperature, the stronger oxidation and etching of GQDs have greatly reduced the particle size of GQDs, therefore resulted in red-shifting of emission band. Moreover, the GQDs became less emissive as the particle size increased. The low fluorescence intensity can be explained by the fewer defect sites per unit area available in the large particle. However, this finding was in opposition to that of Zhou et al. [42] who reported that there was no intercorrelation between the diameter of CQDs and PL emission. They claimed that the random trend of PL emission spectra implied that the emission behavior of CQDs was controlled by surface state instead of the graphitic core size.

In view of the controversial discussion on PL mechanism, some researchers claimed that the dominant PL mechanism of fluorescent carbon materials is the synergistic effect of the two factors [91,119]. In the work by Wang et al. [120], a series of carbon quantum dots with undefined emission ranging from blue, red to even white light were fabricated. The detailed studies proved that the particle sizes and PL wavelengths of CQDs can be modulated over a wide range by choosing the suitable acid reagent for the synthesis process. The acid reagents were adopted to passivate the CQDs surface and introduced different amounts of electron-withdrawing groups on CQDs surface. This can effectively modulate the degree of graphitization and control the particle sizes of CQDs. Since the particle size of CQDs is positively related to the surface groups, it can be elucidated that both the quantum size effect and surface chemistry are interconnected and are very important for the tunable emission of CQDs.

Similarly, Liu et al. [121] reported that the PL properties of CQDs can be coarsely adjusted by quantum confinement effect (QCE) and finely tuned by surface trap states (STS). Although the CQDs own the same core size, different amounts of surface defects have been found on the surface which leads to a slight shifting of the PL wavelength. In such case, the particle size of CQDs plays a more significant role in adjusting the band gap than the surface functional groups do. This is because the PL center is mainly located inside the CQDs as evidenced by the slight red-shift of about 5 nm with no intensity change after the addition of H2O2. This has proven that the surface functional groups have been oxidized but the π-conjugated carbon core is less susceptible to damage. The stability characterization further indicated that the core structure is located at a relative inside position, therefore QCE can enhance the PL to a larger extent than STS. Up till now, there is no universal agreement on the PL mechanisms owing to the various factors involved in the PL emission.

Generally, surface state emission is closely related to the diverse structural defects introduced through surface oxidation and surface functionalization [41,122]. In the past studies, it has been endorsed that bare CQDs without surface modification are often not fluorescent in nature, hence the low quantum yield remains a major challenge to the synthesis of fluorescence CQDs [123]. To increase the QY of CQDs, the surface treatments conducted have been evidenced to tune the PL properties of CQDs [91,124,125]. The surface states act as capture and trap centers for electrons whereby energy will be released in the form of photons upon electron relaxation and recombination [118].

To make the phenomenon of PL shifting clear and understandable, the electronic transition in CQDs molecules has been studied in depth. Generally, electron transition in CQDs can happen in two pathways, which are the n–π* and π–π* transitions. The π-states are associated with the conjugated carbon in the CQDs core while the n-states are ascribed to the sp3 sites or the functional groups with lone pair of electrons [41]. The n–π* interstates can be formed when the functional groups are bonded to the aromatic π states, thereby introducing new intermediate states (n-states) within the π–π* energy gap [126]. The tunability of CQDs photoluminescence is said to be originated from the n–π* interstate imparted during surface modulation [89].

According to Tang et al. [92], the quantum yield of CQDs can be enhanced to different extents depending on the doping elements such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and phosphorus (P) hereatoms. The significant enhancement in the fluorescence of CQDs was ascribed to the reduction of non-emissive carboxylic groups (‒COOH) attaching at the edges of sp2 aromatic domain [117]. Upon surface modulation, the ‒COOH groups was converted to carbonyl (‒C˭O), amines, amides and thiols groups which considerably reduced the surface dangling bonds that tend to cause non-radiation relaxation [127]. By stabilizing the surface dangling bonds, the electron transition can be directed towards a radiative fluorescence pathway, hence doped-CQDs are collectively more emissive than the bare CQDs.

Besides passivating the CQDs surface, the dopants can also induce new emissive states to the CQDs surface, hence leading to multi-color emission [92]. Fig. 2 shows the excitation-dependent emission behavior of CQDs in which the emission color depends on the transition pathways of excitons which can be adjusted by imparting different n-state energy levels within the π–π* band. The selection of suitable dopant is important since it has a large impact on the shifting of emission spectra. Generally, elements with higher electronegativity will induce a higher energy state within the π–π* energy gap. The stronger electron-withdrawing effect eventually lead to blue-shifting in PL spectra [110]. For example, n-type dopants (providing extra electrons) with high electronegativity such as N (3.04) and S (2.58) atoms will induce a high energy state within the π–π* gap, while the p-type atoms (donating extra holes) with low electronegativity such as P (2.19) and B (2.04) atoms have a more pronounced electron donor effect [110]. Li et al. reported that the N atoms with strong electron-withdrawing effect tend to attract electrons to itself while the large width of energy gap gives rise to higher excitation energy, hence leading to a blue-shifted PL spectrum. On the contrary, the electronic donor effect and lower light absorption energy of the dopant with lower electronegativity favor red-shifted spectra.

With reference to the work by Wang et al.[128], four kinds of hereatoms-doped GQDs (N/S/P/B) were studied to tailor the quantum yield and photoluminescence properties of GQDs. The experimental results indicated that the emission wavelength of both N-GQDs (443.6 nm) and S-GQDs (447.8 nm) were blue-shifted while the emission peak of P-GQDs (526.8 nm) and B-GQDs (525.2 nm) were red-shifted. The drastic red-shift of emission peak of P-GQDs and B-GQDs can be explained by the high defects sites being introduced into the graphitic carbon framework, and thus reduced the PL emission induced by radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the PL quantum yield of GQDs increased in the order of GQDs < S-GQDs < P-GQDs < B-GQDs < N-GQDs which were 0.8%, 1.28%, 1.52% 1.68% and 15.8%, respectively. Generally, all the doped-GQDs showed improved QY than the pure GQDs. Therefore, surface modulation of CQDs by hereatom doping has become the main strategy to fine-tune the PL properties and improve the QY of carbon nanomaterials.

Furthermore, many studies have revealed that the PL behavior of CQDs is highly associated with the amount of oxygen-containing moieties on CQDs [122]. The degree of surface oxidation that varies with the synthesis approach would determine the number of oxygenated groups produced during partial decomposition of the C˭C conjugated structure [41]. A greater amount of structural defects can be formed under a higher degree of surface oxidation [113]. The surface enriched with oxygen impurities would increase the chances of exciton trapping and radiative recombination. Fig. 3 also shows that a higher oxygen content would reduce the band gap of CQDs, hence giving rise to the red-shifting of emission spectra [113,117]. Due to their flexible tunability, CQDs as excellent functional materials have been extensively modified to suit their purpose under particular conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifaceted roles of CQDs in hybrid photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The great potential of CQDs for the design of high-performance photocatalysts can be accredited to their multiple promoting effects in the photocatalytic system. Being a highly versatile material, CQDs can serve as an electron mediator, spectral converter, adsorption center, photosensitizer, and sole photocatalyst [4,41,52]. In most cases, these multifarious effects of CQDs often occur simultaneously. The roles performed by CQDs may differ according to the region of light irradiation. In ultraviolet (UV) region, CQDs display electron-sink behavior which helps in trapping scattered electrons released by metal oxides. In the event of visible-near infrared (NIR) radiation, the outstanding UCPL and photosensitization properties of CQDs enable them to serve as visible-NIR light harvester and co-catalyst, respectively. Also, CQDs can function concurrently as electron acceptors which enable the transfer of photo-excited electrons away from their donors [41].

By compositing CQDs with bulk semiconductors, the optical properties of resulting composites have been successfully tuned which can be verified by the extended absorption edge to the visible region. The tail-extending is originated from the band gap narrowing owing to the introduction of a new carbon energy state between the π states of CQDs and the conduction band of semiconductors during electronic coupling [71,130]. In this section, the multifaceted functions of CQDs will be explained in detail and the cooperative photocatalytic reaction mechanisms of CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 4.

The high relaxation and recombination rate of charge carriers is presumably due to the random defects on the photocatalysts surfaces which act as recombination centers for free moving electrons [130]. Over the past decade, massive efforts have been directed towards the integration of narrow band gap semiconductors with noble metals [131], scavengers [132], carbon-based supports [133] and other semiconductors with different electronic states [134] to modify their intrinsic band structure.

In recent years, nanostructured CQDs were extensively used in the modification of semiconductors taking advantage of their large electron-storing capacity and excellent electron-accepting ability [135]. The strong electron affinity of CQDs tends to direct the flow of electrons across the heterojunction of dyade structure. As a result, The electrons then shuttle freely in the conducting network of CQDs which separates them from the holes that stay on the valence band of semiconductor [136]. The high-speed separation prevents them from returning to the equilibrium state, thereby realizing charge stabilization in the hybrids [4,41]. The prolonged lifetime of charge carriers can guarantee their involvement in the generation of free radicals and eventually improve the overall degradation efficiency of photocatalysts. Moreover, the rapid charge separation can protect the semiconductor from photocorrosion and therefore enhance the structural stability of metal-based photocatalysts [137].

Due to poor exploitation of solar light by the wide band gap semiconductors, narrow band gap metal oxides such as hematite [138] and bismuth molybdenum oxide [139] are often employed in the photodegradation reaction for better harvesting of solar energy. However, their practical applicability is greatly restricted by low stability, high toxicity and poor separation of charge carriers. Therefore, CQDs are deemed as a robust candidate for the absorption of broad-spectrum light thanks to their low toxicity and high chemical stability. Other than the intrinsic down-conversion PL property, CQDs also exhibit unique UCPL behavior which enables the conversion of long-wavelength excitation source to short-wavelength light [2]. Thereafter, the converted lights carrying high photon energy can be absorbed by the neighboring semiconductor. The expanded photoresponse of semiconductor therefore promotes more excited electrons to participate in photocatalytic reaction [140,141].

Although the UCPL characteristic of CQDs has been extensively studied over the past decade, the mechanism behind this phenomenon still remains ambiguous. It was reported earlier that UCPL is regarded as a multi-photon active process that involves sequential absorption of two or more low energy photons for high energy emission [29,142]. However, this theory is later opposed by Shen et al. [143] who described UCPL as an anti-Stokes type transition in which the higher energy emission is due to the constant energy difference between the excitation and emission light. This phenomenon can be explained using the schematic diagram of the electron transition process illustrated in Fig. 5. During vibrational energy relaxation, π-electrons that are excited to LUMO π* orbitals would transit back to HOMO σ orbital. The σ orbital that lies below the intermediate π orbital causes an energy difference that corresponds to the excess energy in the emitted light [123]. Although CQDs could emit a series of short-wavelength lights, only a fraction of those can be utilized during the excitation of semiconductor, depending on the absorption edge of the hybrid photocatalyst [144].

The adsorption capability of CQDs towards the target pollutants also play an important role in the photodegradation reaction. As a nanosized material, CQDs with a high surface area to volume ratio could provide additional surface active sites for the interfacial adhesion of pollutants [145]. Generally, the adsorption mechanisms can be categorized into physisorption and chemisorption in which the interaction mode differs from the types of reactant species (molecules, atoms, ions) [2]. For organic adsorbates, the π–π stacking interaction between the conjugated sp2 carbon structure of CQDs and the aromatic rings of organic pollutants could enhance their molecular contact in aqueous solution [100,138,140]. On the other hand, the considerable amount of functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, carboxyl, amino and epoxy groups) that present on the surface of CQDs would increase their interaction with a diverse range of molecules and heavy metal ions, hence leading to enhanced adsorption efficiency [146]. The adsorbed pollutants on the CQDs surface can be immediately oxidized by the free-radicals generated, thus speeding up the overall rate of photodegradation.

Nowadays, photosensitizers are commonly used to assist in the process of visible-light-driven photocatalysis by extending the photoresponse range of wide band gap metal oxides [147,148]. Photosensitization is a photo-excitation process mediated by light-absorption molecules that eventually bring chemical changes to the substrate molecules [2]. Over the past decade, CQDs have been widely utilized as a promising light-harvesting photosensitizer due to their excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity and high availability [141,149,150]. By virtue of the attractive merits of CQDs, conventional photosensitizers such as organic dyes, metal complexes and inorganic sensitizers have been substituted since they are expensive, unstable and highly toxic, thus limiting their practical uses in aqueous solution [41,145]. During the sensitization process, CQDs will harvest low-energy photons to excite electrons from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level [73]. The chemical bond between the heterostructure would facilitate the charge transfer from the LUMO level of sensitizer to the conduction band of the metal oxide substrate, thus rendering these host photocatalysts visible-light photoactive. For example, in the study by Liang et al.[151], the photocatalytic activity of ZnO sensitized with L-CQDs was 60% higher than that of bare ZnO. As evidenced in the UV–vis DRS study, the bulk ZnO has almost no light absorption at wavelength greater than 500 nm. The sensitization effect of L-CQDs has significantly extended the photoresponse range to the perceivable region (up to 700 nm). Moreover, the reduced band gap energy of 2.96 eV manifested that the L-CQDs can be excited by photons with energy greater than or equal to the band gap energy. This indicated that the L-CQDs can be used as photosensitizers which increased the electronic density of the bulk semiconductor and in turn improved the photocatalytic activity.

Although CQDs are often found in conjunction with metal oxides to address the problem of rapid charge recombination, the direct use of CQDs alone as photocatalysts has shown great potential in the degradation of pollutants. Table 3 summarizes the latest studies that utilized CQDs as sole photocatalyst in photodegradation reaction.

In the work of Zhou et al. [42], CDs were synthesized via a microwave-mediated reaction. The CDs were separated into three different sizes to study the influence of particle size on catalytic performance. For the first time, the CDs displayed size-dependent photocatalytic activity in the degradation of dye pollutants. The bare CDs without post-treatment can drive photocatalytic reaction and their performance can be further enhanced by decreasing the particle size. Although all sizes of CDs shared the same functional groups, the relative abundance of O element was found the highest in the small-sized CDs. This is because the surface rich in O elements introduced more impurity levels into the electronic structure which narrowed the n–π* gap. The reduced band gap of CDs eased the visible light absorption and eventually resulted in high photocatalytic activity. Also, the size-independent emission of CDs revealed that the PL mechanism of CDs did not correlate with the quantum confinement effect.

Recently, Aghamali et al. [48] synthesized N-doped CQDs by which the fabrication of CQDs and surface passivation were accomplished concurrently via a one-step hydrothermal method. Surprisingly, the as-prepared N-CQD displayed an unbelievably high quantum yield up to 95%, which is the highest QY that CQDs has attained so far. The excellent degradation efficiency of methylene blue (MB) (100% within 160 min) has proven the prominent light-harvesting ability and high capacitance of N-CQDs. Besides, it has been highlighted that the photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs is more magnificent under natural sunlight as compared to visible-light irradiation. The unusual performance can be explained by the extraordinary UCPL characteristic of N-CQDs which could make efficient use of the inexhaustible solar light.

Zhu et al. [75] had synthesized S and Cl co-doped Bio-CDs using palm powder as the green carbon source. The co-doping of hereatoms had contributed to the superior photocatalytic degradation of RhB and MB. The remarkable degradation efficiency can be explained by the new supplementary energy levels introduced within the framework. During electron excitation, the extra electronic states induced by the doped elements will capture the photo-excited electrons and retard the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Moreover, doped CDs are less subjected to aggregation benefiting from the reduced surface energy of Bio-CDs. This could in turn increase the surface contact with target pollutants during the photocatalytic reaction. Despite their good electron-trapping capability, Bio-CDs showed a degraded PL property with low quantum yield (0.9%), which will be discussed below.

According to the findings above, it can be observed that the photocatalytic performance of CQD is significantly influenced by their surface chemistry. Generally, different surface functional groups may affect the direction and degree of band bending within the nanostructure. Band bending could induce an internal electric field which promotes faster migration of charge carriers. Hu et al. [152] highlighted that carbonyl and carboxylic groups can induce higher intensity of upper band bending whereas hydroxyl groups can only cause a slight upward bending, which hardly assists in the separation of charge carriers. In their later study, Hu et al. [153] had proven that carbonyl group is the main catalytic active site among the other oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid). Therefore by increasing the concentration of carbonyl groups on the CDs surface, the photocatalytic activity of CDs can be significantly enhanced.

Furthermore, tailoring of CQDs surface with different hereatoms may lead to different promoting effects. In the work of Aghamali et al. [48], the N-CQDs possess excellent PL property which can be accredited to the presence of numerous amide and carboxyl groups. The multiple energy levels split between the n–π* gap are responsible for the trapping and transition of electrons from the HOMO to LUMO states, thus leading to multi-color emission upon radiative recombination. In the case of Zhu et al. [75], the low QY of Bio-CDs can be explained by the retarded recombination of excited electrons in S, Cl-doped Bio-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6(a), part of the excited electrons was being trapped by the S, Cl-related defect states. This resulted in a change in electron transition pathways which restricted the excited electrons from recombining with the holes that resided on the π states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational designing of CQD supported photocatalysts/CQDs as co-catalyst
Design of binary composite of CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has garnered tremendous attention in photocatalysis due to its relatively small band gap (2.7 eV), superior thermal stability and chemical durability, facile surface modification technique and high abundance on earth [161]. As a metal-free semiconductor, the polymeric nature of g-C3N4 poses no risk of free metal ion leaching as compared to other visible-light-active metal oxides [3]. Nevertheless, the practical application of g-C3N4 in photocatalysis is still hampered by its rapid charge recombination, low quantum efficiency and low specific surface area [162]. Therefore, it is indispensable to modify the bulky structure of g-C3N4 in order to ameliorate their photocatalytic performance. Table 4 summarizes the CQDs-modified g-C3N4 photocatalysts and their respective photocatalytic application in the past five years.

In a recent study conducted by Wong et al., CQDs have been incorporated on g-C3N4 (CNQDs) via an in-situ hydrothermal treatment [163]. As a result, the CNQDs with enhanced charge separation ability showed a higher photodegradation efficiency of bisphenol A (BPA) than the exfoliated g-C3N4 (GCN) alone. The PL intensity of GCN reduced by 30% after thermal exfoliation while the PL peak of CNQD was further weakened by 80%. The weakening of PL intensity in GCN corresponded to the electron-confining and electron-withdrawing effects induced by the 2D nanostructure and the existence of oxygenated groups, respectively. On the other hand, the rapid PL quenching in CNQD implied the enhanced separation of charge carriers by CQDs.

Besides, the photo-reactivity of CNQDs was studied under varied hydrothermal temperatures (100–200 °C) and duration (0–20 h). It was found that the CNQDs treated at high temperatures (160 °C and above) showed an exponential removal rate whereas beyond 180 °C, the degradation rate declined tremendously. A similar trend was observed for the effect of hydrothermal duration in which the optimal duration was found at 12 h. The author concluded that the hydrothermal treatment conducted under extreme temperature and prolonged duration was not preferable since excessive oxidation has destructed the melon structure of the bulk GCN. Moreover, the structural defects induced by long hours treatment served as a shallow trap for free moving electrons. The electron-trapping effect severely hampered the transition of electrons to the GCN surface and promoted rapid recombination of the trapped electrons with free holes, hence resulted in a short PL lifetime and low kinetic rate.

Apart from this, different hybridization routes in combining g-C3N4 with CQDs have been explored in the study by Seng et al. [164], which included mechanical mixing, thermal polymerization, and hydrothermal treatment. Among the synthesis methods studied, CQDs incorporated through the hydrothermal method exhibited strong physical interaction with the host catalyst which restricted the loss of CQDs during the reaction process. Contrarily, the poor catalytic performance of the mechanically mixed hybrid was ascribed to the loosely-bound NCQD onto the g-C3N4 sheets. While for the thermal polymerization method, although NCQD was impregnated on the g-C3N4 structure, the high reaction temperature greatly reduced the thermal stability of the composites. As a result, the hybrid composites suffered from severe aggregation and experienced a significant weight loss due to decarboxylation and dehydroxylation of the catalyst surface.

Different mass loading of NCQD in the hybrid composite was also studied. The optimal loading was determined at 1 wt% while further increment of NCQD amount rendered these composites with low degradation efficiency. Similar findings were also observed by Di et al. [50] and Wang et al. [162] who reported that the excessive CQDs competed against one another for photons absorption and brought negative inner filter effect to g-C3N4. In addition, the redundant amount of CQDs resulted in the masking of catalyst pores, leading to shielding effect which lowered the light absorption efficiency of photocatalyst. Determining the appropriate amount of CQDs in the heterojunction system is therefore important to harness the full potential of CQD in photocatalytic reaction.

Moreover, another study has reported the use of homogeneous thermal pyrolysis method to fabricate a 0D/2D CQDs/g-C3N4 heterostructure [50]. In this study, the effect of excitation wavelength and light intensity on the photodegradation of sulfamethazine (SMZ) has been studied comprehensively. The experiment was conducted under polychromatic light (380 ≤ λ ≤ 780 nm) and a series of monochromatic light (395–500 nm) with varying light intensity. Notably, CQDs performed their roles differently and presented opposite impact under 3 distinct regions of simulated daylight, namely the inhibition region (380 ≤ λ ≤ 410 nm), transition region (410 < λ ≤ 425 nm) and promotion region (425 < λ ≤ 780 nm). Surprisingly, the promotion effect of CQDs was found at longer wavelength light. Such oscillating behavior of CQDs was due to their down-conversion property which converted short-wavelength light to long-wavelength, thereby causing g-C3N4 to harvest less light irradiation. The down-conversion property of CQDs should be largely suppressed to avoid competition with the other positive effects of CQDs. Besides, the effect of radiation intensity revealed that over-illumination of light has not resulted in higher degradation efficiency. As more charge carriers were generated under high irradiation, the recombination rate of the excess carriers increased as well, hence causing the kinetics to reach an equilibrium state. In this regard, the optimal excitation wavelength and the respective irradiation intensity should be determined in order to achieve maximum efficiency with minimum energy consumption.

Among all the light-responsive semiconductors including ZnO, TiO2, and g-C3N4, TiO2 has been extensively studied due to its outstanding optical and electronic properties [174]. To date, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light irradiation is still limited due to some unresolved technical barriers. One of the common setbacks is the low yield of free radicals due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. Besides, the narrow light absorption spectra of TiO2 associated with its intrinsic large bandgap (anatase, ~ 3.2 eV) also limit the further application of TiO2 [71]. To greatly abate the shortages of TiO2, the conjugation of TiO2 with CQDs has been widely adopted in numerous studies. Table 5 compiles the latest CQDs-modified TiO2 photocatalysts with their photocatalytic performances in water disinfection.

A recent study by Wongso et al. [109] demonstrated that acetaminophen can be degraded by coupling TiO2 nanorod with silica doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQD) synthesized from waste rice husk. With the introduction of Si-CQDs, the limiting barriers of TiO2 were overcome owing to the synergistic roles of Si-CQD playing as electron collector and photosensitizer in the hybrid photocatalyst. Although the optical band gap of TiO2 reduced from 3.2 eV to 3.12 eV, the slight reduction was insufficient for TiO2 to make use of the natural sunlight. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance was mainly attributed to the photosensitization feature of Si-CQDs which harvested visible spectrum and initiated charge excitation during the removal of acetaminophen. In addition, the increased BET surface area of the CQD-modified composite (126 m2/g to 148 m2/g) provided more catalytic spots for the adsorption of reactant molecules. The mutual combination effect of CQD in enhancing the visible light absorption, electron transferability and adsorption capacity of photocatalyst eventually contributed to the supreme photocatalytic activity of Si-CQDs/TiO2.

Another biomass material that has been selected as an alternative precursor for CQD fabrication is coal tar pitch (CTP). In the work of Zhang et al. [71], the surfaces of CTP dots and TiO2 were linked together through π-electron interaction and Ti‒O‒C bonding. The chemical bonding introduced impurity level within the band structure and provided a new electron transfer channel between CTP and TiO2. In the photodegradation of RhB, the CTP/TiO2 composite demonstrated a superlative degradation rate constant which was 23 times higher than TiO2 alone. The CTP dots not only serve as visible-light harvester but also function as an electron conduit in enhancing the electrical conductivity of charge carriers across the heterojunction interface of nanocomposites. Meanwhile, our group has developed an oil palm frond-derived NCQDs/TiO2 and was adopted for the first time in the photodegradation of Methylene Blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. The NCQDs/TiO2-1 nanocomposite showed a comparable performance to the CTP/TiO2 composite synthesized from waste coal tar pitch, which degraded 40.93% of MB within 1 h of visible-light irradiation [175].

Considering the higher surface energy (0.9 J/m2) of TiO2 (001) facets, the design of NCDs/TNS-001 heterostructure via a simple mixing process has been attempted by Wang et al. [141]. The low defect density on 001 facets directed the preferential flow of photo-induced electrons over the grain boundaries, thereby reducing the electron transfer resistance. It was also postulated that the reactive 001 facets allowed a higher affinity of dye molecules to the TiO2 surface. Besides tuning the surface facets of TiO2, the integration of TiO2 with NCDs enabled the spontaneous separation of charge carriers and at the same time, narrowed the band gap energy of composite for broader spectrum absorption. Notably, the photocatalytic activity of NCDs/TNS-001 was 6 times more intense than the bulk TiO2 nanosheet within the same time frame. The robust photocatalytic activity was attributed to the cumulative impacts imposed by the high oxidation rate of exposed (001) facets and the efficient Z-scheme charge transmission system.

ZnO has been regarded as a substitute photocatalyst for TiO2 thanks to its low toxicity, high biocompatibility and excellent physiochemical stability [43]. Besides, the production cost of ZnO is relatively cheaper than that of TiO2 which makes it more suitable for large-scale production [187]. Although ZnO is famous for its antimicrobial and photodegradation activity, the wide band gap energy (3.37 eV) and the rapid charge recombination rate of ZnO are unfavorable to the photocatalytic application under visible light [3,72]. Therefore, by conducting appropriate band gap modification, the structural properties can be tuned and thereby making them more feasible for photocatalytic degradation. Table 6 summarizes the application of CQDs/ZnO composites in the photodegradation of various dye pollutants.

In the work of Li et al. [188], CQDs were fabricated through a modified microwave method and were hydrothermally mixed with ZnO nanoparticles to form CQDs/ZnO hybrid photocatalysts. The surface morphology of the prepared hybrid samples displayed a burger-like cluster structure that suffered from severe aggregation. The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) by CQDs/ZnO was thereby retarded since the aggregation of photocatalyst blocked the active sites from light absorption. After being homogeneously dispersed on halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) ( Fig. 7), the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs was enhanced by 28% owing to the homogeneous dispersion of nanoparticles on the HNTs carriers. In this work, CQDs not only serve as an electron reservoir but also assist in the tuning of band position so that ZnO can make effective exploitation of low-energy excitation wavelength. The modification of band position is crucial as it certainly decides the kind of reactive species generated and their respective strength for redox reaction.

Likewise, in the work by Liang et al. [151], the conduction band of ZnO was shifted below the reduction level of superoxide anion while the valence band became more positive after hybridized with cationic CQDs (L-CQDs). The pivotal role of L-CQD was to assist ZnO nanoparticles in adjusting their band edges and broadening their spectral response range to the visible region. The stronger reduction and oxidation potentials were conducive for the generation of reactive oxygen species. Together with the efficient interfacial migration of charges, ZnO sensitized by L-CQDs has successfully achieved a remarkable performance in the removal of phenol.

The enhanced performance of CQDs-modified ZnO has also been validated in the study by Qu et al. [68]. The photocatalytic performance of ZnO/N,S-CQDs nanosheet-constructed nanoflower was evaluated under simulated sunlight irradiation using refractory antibiotics as model pollutants. Within 20 min of light irradiation, 92.9% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was degraded while 86.7% of cephalexin (CEL) was decomposed after 50 min. The superior stability and recyclability of the hybrid nanoflower were proven by the negligible loss in degradation efficiency after being recycled for 5 times. Herein, it can be said that the N,S-CQDs were tightly embedded within the ZnO nanoflowers ( Fig. 8) which impeded the leaching of N,S-CQD during the degradation reaction.

To study the effect of hereatom doping of CQDs on the structural change of ZnO, bare CQD without surface modification was produced, which denoted as ZnO/b-CQDs. In the absence of surface functionalization, the free hydroxyl groups on b-CQD competed with hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) agent to form hydrogen bonds with the superficial active sites on ZnO. HMTA is a covering agent needed for the growth of nanosheet structure which is a highly reactive facet. Fig. 9(a) compares the structural mechanisms of ZnO/N,S-CQDs and ZnO/b-CQDs. Due to the reduced linkage of HMTA on ZnO, the HMTA did not work adequately and the composite structure started to change from nanosheet to spindle nanorods. The ZnO/b-CQDs experienced a tremendous drop in degradation efficiency as depicted in Fig. 9(b). The above explanation was also applicable to the high mass ratio of N,S-CQDs to ZnO. When the surface coverage of N,S-CQDs exceeded a certain degree, nanorods-composed nanoflowers would be formed since lesser HMTA could attach to the ZnO surface. On this account, doping of CQDs and adding an appropriate content of CQDs are both essential for the construction of nanosheet-constructed nanoflower which is more energetic than the nanorod form.

Among the metal oxide semiconductors, iron oxides have gained considerable interest due to their magnetic properties that allow rapid recovery of nano-sized photocatalyst from aqueous suspension [192]. As a transition metal oxide, iron oxide is susceptible to oxidation and can be easily transformed into different phase structures, which include magnetite (Fe3O4), hematite (α-Fe2O3), maghemite (γ-Fe2O3) and goethite (α-FeOOH) [193]. Generally, the magnetic photocatalysts possess favorable band gap energy (2.0–2.2 eV) that allows the harvesting of visible-light in various photocatalytic applications [192]. Unfortunately, iron oxide suffers from high recombination rate of charge carriers owing to its poor electron mobility and conductivity. For this reason, various CQDs-based iron oxide heterostructures have been constructed ( Table 7) to accelerate the surface-charge transfer and optimize solar energy conversion in iron oxide.

According to Li et al. [194], CQD was doped on maghemite surface (γ-Fe2O3) for better activation of persulfate (PDS) and degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). Activation of PDS is an essential step needed for the generation of reactive sulfate radicals (SO4 •−) to destruct the recalcitrant structure of SMX. It was noticed that the kinetic constant of CQDs@γ-Fe2O3/PDS was 31.8 times higher than that of bare γ-Fe2O3/PDS system. The high degradation rate has proven the presence of CQDs acting as localized electron acceptors that extract the electrons generated by metal oxide. Besides, the author proclaimed that the integration of CQD can eliminate the solid-liquid interfacial mass transfer resistance between the photocatalysts and PDS solution, therefore resulting in high catalytic activity towards PDS. As depicted in Fig. 10, the CQDs surface enriched with photo-induced electrons initiated the in-situ generation of surface-bound Fe2+ to activate PDS for SMX degradation. Meanwhile, a heterogeneous iron cycle that occurred on the CQDs surface can regenerate Fe2+ for the continuous degradation of SMX. Eventually, the efficient interfacial generation of surface-bound Fe2+ and regeneration of Fe2+ on CQDs accelerated the Fenton-like reaction as well as the decomposition of SMX in visible light-irradiated system.

Another Fenton-like degradation system was reported by Huang et al. [195] who prepared a CQD incorporated goethite nanohybrids (CQDs/α-FeOOH) via a facile hydrolysis method. The photocatalytic behavior of CQDs/α-FeOOH nanohybrids was studied through the degradation of tetracycline (TC) in the Fenton oxidation system. The efficient degradation of CQDs/α-FeOOH was correlated to the up-converted character of CQDs and the saturation of TC pollutants around the surface of CQDs/α-FeOOH adsorbent. A plausible mechanism for the CQDs/α-FeOOH-catalyzed Fenton oxidation process is depicted in Fig. 11. Upon visible-light illumination, the excited charged carriers on the valence band were promoted to the conduction band of α-FeOOH, followed by the migration of electrons to the neighboring CQDs. The separated electrons then participated in the iron cycle which substantially initiated the activation of H2O2 and generated •OH radicals for the degradation of TC. Compared to the bare α-FeOOH-catalyzed H2O2 system, the CQDs modified Fenton system showed superior enhancement in TC degradation, which was around 3.7 times of α-FeOOH + H2O2 + vis system. Benefiting from the fast electron transportation and enhanced responsiveness of composites towards visible-NIR spectrum, the catalytic performance of nanohybrid in different water matrices upon natural solar irradiation was significantly improved.

Besides, the formation of a magnetic photocatalyst with superior catalytic ability and recyclability can be achieved by attaching C-dots on magnetite Fe3O4 surface. Sun et al. [196] reported the robust photocatalytic ability of C-dots-grafted Fe3O4, which was mainly contributed by the excitation and promotion of electrons in the C-dots bands. In this event, most of the electron-hole pairs were contributed by C-dots even though Fe3O4 could generate them as well. The low yield of charge and the rapid charge recombination of Fe3O4 can therefore be ignored. Nevertheless, the outstanding electronic conductivity of Fe3+ ions on Fe3O4 surface facilitated the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in the composite. As shown in Fig. 12, the superparamagnetic behavior of magnetite Fe3O4 enabled the rapid isolation of these nanocomposites by simply imparting a magnetic field on the reaction solution. This is beneficial for the fast recovery and recycling of catalyst during post-treatment process.

Up to now, different bismuth-based photocatalysts such as dibismuth tetraoxide (Bi2O4), bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6), bismuth molybdenum oxide (Bi2MoO6), bismuth titanate (Bi3Ti4O12) and bismuth oxyhalides, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I), etc. have been explored to prepare visible light-responsive photocatalysts as summarized in Table 8. Compared with the single component semiconductor, Bi-based materials are composed of hybrid valence bands with alternating layered structure. Due to their unique structures, Bi-based materials show amplified photo-response to visible light and exhibit an internal electric field, thus realizing effective charge separation [150].

A direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism has been proposed by Yue et al. [150] for m-Bi2O4/NCDs nanocomposite. A monoclinic Bi2O4 microrod modified with NCDs was constructed via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Compared to m-Bi2O4 alone, m-Bi2O4/NCDs efficiently degraded MO and phenol pollutants within 30 and 120 min. As illustrated in Fig. 13, NCD act as a semiconductor in the reaction. The charge transfer mode in the Z-scheme heterojunction promoted the recombination of electrons at less negative CB of m-Bi2O4 with the holes at VB of NCDs, thereby resulting in an effective spatial separation of electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the accumulation of electrons and holes at the respective band edges with strong redox ability improved the molecular oxygen reduction ability of photocatalyst, thus producing more dominant superoxide in photocatalytic system.

Similarly, bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) with a relatively small band gap has a short carrier transport route which greatly limits their application [199,200]. To depress the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, Zhang et al. [58] prepared a binary N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterostructure by adhering N-CQDs on the surface of sphere-like Bi2WO6 via hydrothermal method. Similar hybrid material was also synthesized by Wang et al. [201] who adhered CQDs on monolayer Bi2WO6 ultrathin nanosheets using the same synthesis method. Both CQDs-deposited Bi2WO6 photocatalysts demonstrated excellent photocatalytic activity than their corresponding bare Bi2WO6. It can be inferred that the efficient crossing of charge carriers across the interface of CQDs and Bi2WO6 accounted for the satisfactory performance in organic pollutants degradation.

The authors suggested a type II mechanism to explain the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the hybrid materials. Firstly, CQDs worked as a potential photocenter which harvested visible and near-infrared light, and converted them to higher energy photon by the UCPL properties. With the tight integration of CQDs, the excited electrons were captured by the LUMO level of CQDs with a higher reduction level. The abundance defect sites on the surface of CQDs were beneficial for the trapping of electrons which ensures high electron intensity for the activation of oxygen molecules. The remarkable versatility of CQDs makes it a promising candidate for the fabrication of visible-light responsive composite with high degradation efficiency.

In another work, Gao et al. [76] had fabricated CQDs from waste larch wood. The as-obtained CQD was then immobilized on bismuth molybdenum oxide (Bi2MoO6) matrix composed of a hollow microsphere structure. The fascinating hollow structure offered a supreme photocatalytic activity for the degradation of organic contaminants. According to the working mechanism depicted in Fig. 14, upon visible light irradiation, the interlayer cavities of the hollow microsphere reflected the incident light and boosted the photon absorption of Bi2MoO6 whenever light travels. However, the rapid photoexcitation process was accompanied by rapid charge recombination in the inner voids and surface of Bi2MoO6. Benefiting from the electronegativity of the oxygen-containing functional groups, CQDs exerted a positive electric field where electrons were attracted to the interface of hybrid composites. The electrons captured on the Bi2MoO6 surface then interacted with the adsorbed oxygen to generate reactive radicals for the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue.

This innovative approach has proven the potential value of the waste wood as a candidate precursor for the green synthesis of CQDs. Similar research was also conducted by Wang et al. [22] who synthesized CQDs from bamboo precursors and integrated the CQDs on bismuth titanate (Bi3Ti4O12) to enhance their photocatalytic activity. Considering the unique features of CQDs and BTO structure, the electron-withdrawing ability of CQDs together with the electric field generated in the inner layer of Bi3Ti4O12 nanosheet can control the interfacial transfer of electrons, thus inhibiting the recombination of bulk and surface electron-hole pairs in the hybrid catalyst.

Besides, numerous studies have established that bismuth oxyhalides, BiOX (X = Cl, Br and I) are a special class of compound with unique layered structure which make it amenable to photocatalytic degradation. The electronegativity difference arises from the intercalated layered structure consisting of positive Bi2O2 slabs sandwiched between 2 slabs of negative halogen layers may give rise to an internal electrostatic field [202,203]. Such structure is beneficial to the mobility of charge carriers which promotes the spatial separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. Among them, BiOBr has been widely studied as demonstrated in the work of Liang et al. [204] and Ji et al. [203]. Both CQDs-modified BiOBr exhibited enhanced removal efficiency of antibiotics and organic pollutants compared to their bare BiOBr counterpart. Furthermore, Liang et al. discovered the BET surface area of CQDs-grafted BiOBr was 16 times larger than that of pristine BiOBr nanosheet, which was mainly attributed to the superior adsorption capacity of CQDs. The increased surface active sites facilitated the interfacial adhesion of pollutant molecules around the photocatalyts, leading to an increased rate of photocatalytic reaction.

Moreover, Ji et al. disclosed that the uniform distribution of CQD on BiOBr matrix was achieved by using CQD stock solution instead of freeze-dried powder during the grafting process. This is because freeze-dried CQDs deactivated the surface functional groups. The reduced surface reactivity of CQD further weakened the interfacial bonding between CQDs and BiOBr nanosheets, thus leading to lower dispersion of CQDs. Besides the BiOBr nanosheet, the BiOCl/CQDs/rGO ternary composite prepared via simple hydrothermal approach exhibited marvelous degradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin (CIP) [64]. Similar findings were reported by Chen’s groups [205] who synthesized CQDs/Bi5OI7 nanorod hybrid via an ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method. The remarkable removal efficiency of CQDs/Bi5OI7 towards BPA was achieved owing to the improved adsorption capacity and visible-light absorption ability of CQDs.

Tungsten trioxide (WO3) is an N-type semiconductor with narrow band gap energy (2.7–28 eV) and good resilience to photocorrosion [212]. WO3 has been extensively applied in photocatalytic oxidation reaction owing to its more positive VB potential with strong oxidizing ability [149]. However, the rapid charge recombination and insufficient CB potential of WO3 have greatly limit the photocatalytic activity of WO3. Therefore, it is imperative to tune the electronic structure in order to enhance the redox ability of WO3 ( Table 9). Yan et al. [212] presented a simple hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of carbon nanodots modified tungsten trioxide (CND/WO3) photocatalyst. The superior catalytic performance of CND/WO3 was evidenced by the rapid decomposition of MB pollutant in a short period (100% in 0.5 h). The high versatility of CND/WO3 photocatalyst was proven by the successful degradation of various organic pollutants (MB, RhB and MO). Doping of CND with WO3 reduced the probability of charge recombination in the network of WO3 thanks to its outstanding electron mediating behavior. The degradation mechanism of CND/WO3 demonstrates a typical type II charge transmission mode whereby the electron transition takes place from the CB of WO3 to that of CND.

On the other hand, a Z-scheme WO3-NCDs composite with excellent catalytic performance in the oxidation of cyclohexane has been reported by Zhang et al. [149]. NCDs were assembled on the surface of WO3 nanosheets through solvothermal reaction. The WO3-NCDs composite showed a high conversion rate of 7.88% and high selectivity to KA oil of 98.9%, which was 1.5 times higher than that of bare WO3 nanosheets. Upon visible-light irradiation, both WO3 and NCDs were photo-excited simultaneously as depicted in Fig. 15. Unlike the type II charge transfer mode, the electrons on CB of WO3 were quenched and they recombined with the holes on VB of NCDs in the Z-scheme mode. Since it has been proclaimed that WO3 exhibited a poor reducing ability, therefore, in this case, NCD with a strong reduction potential generated most of the superoxide radicals required for the cyclohexane oxidation. By forming the effective Z-scheme mechanism, the key challenges faced by WO3 which included the high interfacial charge transfer resistance and the poor redox ability of WO3 have been overcome and resulted in an accelerated chain reaction of cyclohexane oxidation.

Recently, a complex composite WO3/GO/NCQDs was designed by Jamila et al. [213] for the photocatalytic degradation of MO under visible light exposure. In this approach, the GO/WO3 nanosheet modified with NCQDs induced a large number of surface defects which were beneficial to the separation of photo-excited electrons. The carbon materials (both GO and NCQDs) with good electron conductivity and mobility immediately scavenged the excited electrons. Moreover, it was postulated that the existence of GO and NCQDs can tune the band gap energy of WO3 by inserting a new valence band into the host matrix. Fig. 16 demonstrates the band gap of WO3 in composite with GO reduced considerably from 2.8 eV to 2.5 eV due to the hybridization of C2p and O2p orbitals that are similar in energy. The band gap energy was further reduced to 2.23 eV with the addition of NCQDs through energy-favored reaction. The modified valence band of WO3 eventually led to a stronger reduction potential. Although GO suppressed the recombination of charge and provided a large surface area for dye adsorption, the degradation rate was comparably lower than that of WO3/GO/NCQDs. NCQDs not only improve the charge transmission ability of composite but also show strong light absorption in the visible region. Therefore, it can be concluded that the remarkable degradation efficiency of the nanohybrid was mainly acquired by the combined effects of NCQDs and GO materials.

Copper oxide can exist in two different forms which are cupric oxide (CuO) and cuprous oxide (Cu2O). CuO is a p-type semiconductor with narrow band gap energy of 1.2–1.4 eV whereas Cu2O is a direct band gap semiconductor with an optical band gap of 2.0–2.1 eV [214]. The excellent photocatalytic ability of both CuO and Cu2O is correlated to their optimal band gap energy which is highly favorable for their practical use in visible-light driven photocatalysis. It has been elucidated that the stability and photoreactivity of copper oxide nanomaterials vary with their sizes and morphologies [215]. In light of this, various morphologies of CuO nanostructures including nanoflakes, nanoneedles, nanoleaves and nanowires have been studied [60]. Facet control of copper oxide can be a matter of great concern as the photocatalytic activity differs depending on the exposed facets of photocatalysts [107,216].

Jamila et al. [216] developed an effective CuO/NCQDs hybrid heterostructure by incorporating NCQDs on CuO nanoleaves through a one-step hydrothermal method. The enhanced photocatalytic activity attained confirmed that the band gap can be tuned by changing the geometry of CuO and the incorporation of small CQDs nanoparticles.
