As depicted in Fig. 17, bulk CuO with an intrinsic narrow band gap energy (1.2 eV) and poor redox potential was incapable to generate free radicals. After the formation of CuO nanoleaves, the smaller-sized particles exhibited an indirect band gap (1.8 eV) that was larger than that of bulk CuO. The addition of NCQDs further increased the indirect band gap energy to 2.0 eV due to the quantum confinement effect. The CuO/NCQDs composite met the minimum band gap requirement for the production of hydroxyl radicals needed for MO degradation. Also, the increased surface roughness of CuO/NCQDs provided more active sites for the degradation of organic pollutants. The strong electronic interaction between the CuO and NCQDs particles responsible for the electron-trapping ability of hybrid material which assured the participation of charge carriers in the redox reaction.

In order to solve the issues of photocorrosion and the fast carrier recombination of Cu2O during the degradation process, Kulandaivalu et al. [107] synthesized CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite through a wet precipitation method. The structural stability and catalytic ability of the resulting composite were evaluated in the gas phase photoreduction of CO2 under visible-light irradiation. It was observed that the CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst demonstrated considerable improvement in the photoreduction of CO2 to ethane. Although Cu2O alone is capable of catalysing CO2 reduction, the hybrid was found more reactive in O2 production. This is because the smaller band gap value (2.2 eV) of Cu2O only enables a small portion of photo-generated electrons to participate in the redox reaction. In view of this, the enhancement of photoactivity in CQDs/Cu2O composite was accredited to the effective role of CQDs serving as electron collectors and transporters which assisted the separation of charges across the interphase of CQDs/Cu2O. In the meantime, the extraction of extra electrons from the Cu2O surface lowered the risk of self-reduction during the catalytic reaction.

Lately, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have drawn increasing attention in this domain because of their unique 2D configuration that resembles a sandwich structure [217]. Being parts of the 2D-layered family, TMDs nanosheets such as molybdenum diselenide (MoSe2) offers strategic opportunity in the field of visible light-driven photocatalysis owing to its adjustable electronic structure and large specific surface area [218]. Moreover, MoSe2 with an extremely low band gap energy (1.2 eV) can afford a higher optical absorption ability. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic activity of MoSe2 is still finite due to the accumulation of electrons on the surface of catalyst and the narrow band gap energy of MoSe2 [65]. Therefore, the idea of integrating MoSe2 with CQDs was inspired to rapidly transfer the electrons away from the catalyst surface and thereby improve the catalytic activity. Ren et al. [65] had successfully synthesized a full-spectrum-responsive photocatalyst by coupling CQDs with MoSe2 via a solvothermal method. The CQDs decorated-MoSe2 hybrid composite (MC) displayed extended lifetime of charge carriers and strong absorption of a variety of light regimes (UV, visible and NIR light illumination), which ascertained the superior performance of Cr(VI) reduction within 180 min. Benefiting from the merits of CQDs, MoSe2 was easily excited and the photo-induced electron-hole pairs were separated effectively. The high availability of charge carriers eventually led to rapid photo-reduction of Cr(VI) into less toxic Cr(III).

Silver orthophosphate (Ag3PO4), which is a well-known visible-light active material is preferred for the decomposition of toxic organic pollutants under visible light [219]. Unfortunately, the fact that Ag3PO4 exhibits low solubility and dissolution in reaction medium has greatly impacted their degradation efficiency as the low exposure of catalyst sites might lead to insufficient contact and adsorption of pollutants onto the surface. Moreover, the formation of metallic Ag particles during the photocatalytic process might cause serious photocorrosion problem that hampers their long term stability in the photocatalytic application. In addition, the black Ag particles might inevitably block the penetration of light and reduce the visible-light absorption of Ag3PO4 [220]. In light of these facts, CQDs as a non-metal element can be anchored on Ag3PO4 to improve their structural stability and photocatalytic performance.

According to Chen et al. [219], NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex was synthesized via a facile precipitation method. As compared to bare Ag3PO4, the incorporation of nitrogen-doped CQD onto Ag3PO4 led to a significant increase in degradation activity under visible light conditions (Table 9). Fig. 18 demonstrates the working mechanism of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 in the photocatalytic process. During the degradation process, metallic Ag particles were released continuously to the reaction solution due to the serious photocorrosion of Ag3PO4. The free metal particles formed a Schottky junction in the photocatalytic system which trapped and prolonged the life-time of photo-excited electrons.

During energy excitation, photo-induced electrons that accumulated on the surface of Ag3PO4 migrated to the LUMO level of NCQDs due to their lower CB position. Meanwhile, electrons was transferred to Ag metals due to the more negative Fermi level of Ag particles. The transfer of electrons across the composite system eventually generated an electromagnetic field which promoted the effective separation of electron-hole pairs. Due to the poor redox potential of Ag3PO4, the generation of superoxide radicals took place on the Ag surface while the remaining holes on the VB of Ag3PO4 directly participated in the degradation reaction. The enhanced performance of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 was attributed to the collective effect of the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of silver nanoparticles and the electron-mediating feature of NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of a ternary hybrid semiconductor composites
ce:section.ce:para: In the previous section, the photocatalytic applications of CQDs are all in conjunction with one type of semiconductor. Although the construction of binary heterostructure has proved to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the bulk semiconductor, the performances of the nanostructured binary systems are still not sufficient to satisfy the visible-light driven photocatalytic degradation. To address the issues related to the limited spectral response to visible-light, noble metals such as Ag, Au and Cu nanoparticles have been loaded on the surface of semiconductor to enhance the light-harvesting scope through surface plasmon response (SPR) effect [171,183,209]. According to the study reported by Wang et al. [221], a noble metal-semiconductor composite system (Au/CQDs-TiO2) was proposed to enhance the visible-light absorption via SPR effect and improve the interfacial charge transfer through the Schottky barrier formed at the interface. After the deposition of Au NPs, the absorption activity of Au-TiO2 NRs and Au/CQDs-TiO2 NRs largely increased which verified the plasmon excitation of Au NPs in 500–600 nm. By loading CQDs and Au NPs on the surface, the band gap of TiO2 NRs reduced notably from 2.94 to 2.73 eV, which greatly improved the performance of catalyst. Besides, the employment of Au NPs can also provide high active sites which increased the overall rate of catalytic process. Despite the great potency of metal nanoparticles, the high cost of these precious metals hardly meet the need of large-scale production.

Very recently, fabrication of ternary heterostructure has been developed by coupling CQDs with two types of semiconductors having different band structures to build highly efficient photocatalysts. Some examples of ternary hybrid metal oxides integrated with CQDs are listed in Table 10. For example, bismuth oxyhalides and carbon nitride that possess suitable band potential can be employed as an additional co-catalyst to extend the spectral range of CQDs/ZnO composite. Guo et al. [222] has reported that g-C3N4 hybridized with CDs/ZnO composite exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than their counterparts (pristine g-C3N4, ZnO and g-C3N4/ZnO). On the other hand, Zarezadeh et al. [223] had synthesized a ternary heterostructure ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots photocatalyst which demonstrated outstanding performance in the decolorization of RhB under visible light. As reported, the simultaneous loading of CQDs and a new member of semiconductor onto the ZnO surface formed a synergy network that combined the benefits of multiple systems. These joint effects are greater than the sum of their individual effects.

Benefiting from the ternary heterojunction formed between ZnO, CQDs and BiOBr or g-C3N4, the electron-transfer resistance of the ternary nanocomposite systems decreased remarkably, which was in line with the increased light-harvesting capability of modified photocatalysts. The binary composites modified with a tertiary VL-responsive semiconductor exhibited excellent vis-NIR absorption in the range of 460–700 nm with absorption intensity higher than their single component and binary counterparts. The enhanced photo-response and fast electron transfer in both ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots and CDs/C3N4/ZnO nanocomposites eventually led to their high photocatalytic activity towards RhB and TC degradation.

The performance of ternary nanocomposites is highly dependent on the weight percentages of ternary additives (BiOBr or g-C3N4) and the amount of CQDs added to ZnO nanoparticles. The kinetic rate constants of the ZnO/BiOBr/C-Dots and CDs/C3N4/ZnO photocatalysts with different loading of CQDs and ternary additives are shown in Fig. 19. Coincidentally, ZnO samples deposited with 30% of BiOBr and 30% of g-C3N4 showed maximal photocatalytic activity in their respective targeted reaction. Further increment in BiOBr or g-C3N4 particles formed large clusters which caused demolition to the heterojunction and slowed down the separation of charge carriers. Meanwhile, 0.25 ml of C-dots and 4 ml of CDs were found to be the optimal amount needed for ZnO/BiOBr and g-C3N4/ZnO composites, respectively. It can be observed that the degradation rate reduced considerably when excess CQDs were added. The reduction in photocatalytic activity was attributed to the shielding effect and the formation of recombination centers induced by the CQDs aggregates. In short, the design of ternary nanocomposites with appropriate content of CQDs and ternary additives is indeed an effective approach to enhance the degradation efficiency of binary composites.

Recently, the fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 and BiOBr/g-C3N4 have been widely attempted to overcome the weak Van der Waals interactions between the g-C3N4 interlayers which greatly restrict the interfacial transfer of electrons [81,224]. Despite the enhanced charge transfer, the binary samples still have the disadvantages of low visible-light absorption. Therefore in another study, Zhang et al. [225] had integrated CQDs/g-C3N4 with BiOBr which possesses an indirect band gap and nanosheet layered structure. The indirect band gap can provide a special charge transport pathway while the unique sandwich structure yielded internal static electric fields that promote the split efficiency of photo-induced electrons. Moreover, the BiOBr nanosheets increased the exposed surface area and active sites available for photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the formation of BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 ternary heterojunction not only retains the negative CB potential (−1.07 eV) of g-C3N4 but also impart a more positive VB position (3.32 eV) of BiOBr to boost the oxidation ability of the photocatalysts.

Although the BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 ternary composite did not broaden the spectra response range of CQDs/g-C3N4, the separation efficiency is over that of the binary hybrid which accounts for its enhancement in the CIP and TC degradation. A graphical representation of the Z-scheme heterojunction is illustrated in Fig. 20. It can be seen that CQDs served as a molecular bridge linking the BiOBr and g-C3N4 semiconductors whereby the excited electrons on the CB of BiOBr were transferred to the CQD mediator and recombined with the free holes on the VB of g-C3N4. In this Z-scheme system, the electrons and holes that assembled at their respective band edges with stronger redox potential undergone a series of redox reactions, thus leading to the high mineralization efficiency of antibiotics.

Lately, Khaneghah et al. [226] had reported a type-II heterojunction ternary system (g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots) which significantly elevated the performance of g-C3N4/C-Dots binary composite. Silicon carbide (SiC), as a non-metallic semiconductor with narrow band gap energy (2.48 eV) and desirable energy levels, is touted as a suitable candidate to collaborate with g-C3N4/C-Dots. The g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots nanocomposite demonstrated an impressive performance in the removal of RhB, which was about 24.4 and 2.2-folds premier than that of bare g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/SiC. The narrow band gap of SiC and the UCPL character of C-dots were the two dominant factors that permitted the effective utilization of visible-light for the excitation of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the strong interface formed between the individual components can speed up the mobility of electrons over the g-C3N4/SiC/C-Dots heterojunction. This research work proposed a type II charge transfer mechanism that is different from the one proposed by Zhang et al. [225]. As seen in Fig. 21, the electrons of g-C3N4 were transferred to CB of SiC using C-dots as mediator while the holes on VB of SiC traveled in the opposite direction. An efficient charge transmission system was achieved by separating the opposite charges into two different regions. However, it has been postulated that the mineralization capability of the type-II charge separation mechanism was less efficient than the Z-scheme heterojunction system [227].

Similar phenomenon was also noticed in the work of Zhang et al. [59] who claimed that the Z-scheme system established in the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalyst can surpass the degradation activity of the traditional type-II heterojunction. This is because in the type-II system, the direction of charge transfer goes against the redox potential of photocatalyts, thereby sacrificing the generation of reactive species. Unlike the type-II system, the charge transmission in the Z-scheme mechanism happens in a way that can retain the redox ability of the heterostructure, hence resulting in a higher yield of free radicals for the mineralization of pollutants.

Another Z-scheme transmission system was reported in the work by Yu et al. [228] who had combined BiWO6/Cu2O composite with a high biocompatibility CQDs. Although the binary system constructed from dual semiconductors (BiWO6/Cu2O) facilitated the spatial separation of charge carriers, the composite structure was not well-designed and had failed to inhibit the excited electrons from leaping back to their ground state. Therefore, CQDs that were introduced between the interfaces of semiconductors provided immediate interfacial separation of charge carriers. The addition of CQDs can gather the photo-generated charge carriers on the band edges with higher redox capability. The modification of BiWO6/Cu2O with CQDs is said to have converted the conventional type II mechanisms into a direct Z-scheme system.

Ever since the first discovery of CQDs in 2004, the application of CQDs in photocatalysis has attracted immense attention in the research community due to their versatility of binding with various bulk semiconductors for the extensive use in solar energy conversion. As such, this review summarized the design strategies for CQDs-based semiconductor photocatalysts and evaluated their recent progress as well as their photocatalytic performance in the degradation of diverse pollutants. This review also reports the diversity of carbon resources used in CQDs fabrication ranging from traditional chemical source to sustainable raw materials. Compared to the chemical-derived CQDs, research on the green synthesis of CQDs is still at an early stage, thereby more efforts should be devoted to explore better synthetic route options from a sustainability perspective. To date, CQDs have unfold multifarious roles in heterogeneous photocatalysis that has been discussed in this review.

Being a robust material, the synergistic effects resulted from the combination of CQDs with semiconductors often exceed that of individual components. According to the review of previous studies, the performance efficiency of photocatalytic systems principally relies on a few technical considerations during the tuning of the band structure and optical properties of composites. The decisive parameters that can be manipulated include surface morphology (size and shape of nanoparticles), crystal structure and exposed facets, band gap and relative band edge position of the semiconductors, surface doping, composition of CQDs in the nanocomposite, synthesis routes selected and their operating conditions applied during the fabrication process.

Despite the intelligent design of CQDs-based photocatalysts and their advancement in catalytic performance, there are still some challenges remain in this domain that are worth solving. The following issues need further investigation before CQDs-based composite could achieve technology maturation in photocatalytic degradation:
1)
Undoubtedly, hydrothermal treatment has been extensively applied in the synthesis of high quality CQDs-based photocatalysts thanks to its facile treatment steps which eliminates the use of strong acids and harsh working conditions. However, the long duration of synthesis process (8–12 h) often requires high energy consumption which is not cost-effective and commercially attractive to mass production. Therefore, finding a competent production method for large scale production of CQDs is still in high demand in this field.
2)
Although numerous CQDs with high QY have been successfully produced, the intricate inner structure and the photoluminescence mechanism of CQDs remain uncertain and are not adequately understood. Moreover, no exact evidence was found to explain the UCPL effect of CQDs. Experimental and theoretical investigation with emphasis on the crystallinity, sizes, morphologies and surface defects of CQDs need to be conducted to search for a convincing explanation on how the tuning of optical properties works.
3)
It is known that the PL properties of CQDs can be tuned and modified by modulating the size, shape and surface functionality of CQDs. However, to date, no well-defined method and technique are available for the precise control of size, shape and population of surface defects of CQDs. Hence, the development of reliable synthesis approach for better control of CQDs properties demands more priorities in all applications.
4)
The photostability of CQDs-based photocatalysts is often being neglected in many studies. After prolonged irradiation and photocatalytic reaction, the oxygenic groups on CQDs surface is reduced to a considerable extent. This issue should be given significant concern since the depleting surface functional groups may bring huge performance impact on their photocatalytic activity. Besides, more attention should be paid for the development of robust CQDs to ensure long-term stability of these photocatalytic systems.
5)
Despite the CQDs play various roles in assisting the photocatalytic activity of composite systems, the direct use of bare CQDs as photocatalyst is rarely studied and has yet to be exploited. Recently, numerous studies showed that CQDs has a great potential to be used as sole photocatalyst due to their narrow band gap between the HOMO and LUMO level. Thus, more fundamental studies and in-depth analysis on the photocatalytic ability of bare CQDs are desired in realizing the full potential of CQDs in photocatalytic reaction.
6)
Lately, nanotechnology has gained considerable popularity in nearly all fields of application due to their high surface area and rapid kinetics. However, the hybrid nanoparticles are highly soluble in water, therefore their application in water remediation possess potential risks on environment and human health. Since the existing management in controlling their entry into water are not well established, post-treatment recovery of spent photocatalyst should be treated seriously to avoid secondary pollution. The associated issues of nanopollution should be resolved either by developing a non-toxic photocatalyst or immobilizing the nanoparticles on different supports such as membrane or magnetic composite. The design of nanocomposites with high recyclability not only could tackle the issues related to secondary pollution, but also allowing them to be reused.

Apart from these recommendations, future studies are encouraged to pay more focus on the synthesis of green photocatalysts that are highly effective. The current challenges and perspectives enable burgeoning development and technological advancement of CQDs in diverse fields of application. Also, this is an opportunity that allows researchers to explore the hidden potential of CQDs on the road towards ultimate achievement. It is hoped that this review could contribute a comprehensive analysis on the rational design of composite photocatalysts and allows reader to get the whole picture of the recent efforts made in optimizing the performance of CQDs-based photocatalyst materials.
